caveat: All figures are contested, lag events on the ground, and mix belligerent claims with independent estimates. Treat every number as an order of magnitude, not a fact.
background: The 2026 Iran war began on 28 February when the United States and Israel launched Operation Epic Fury — coordinated strikes that killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei in the opening hours. Iran retaliated against Israel, US bases, and Gulf states; Hezbollah opened a Lebanon front days later. A memorandum signed 17 June at Versailles and Tehran collapsed on 8 July, and fighting has since re-centered on control of the Strait of Hormuz, which the IRGC declared closed on 12 July.

status.headline: War widens to a second sea as the Houthis threaten Saudi ports and the Pentagon runs short on cash
status.detail: The war opened a second maritime front. Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis escalated their blockade of Saudi Arabia into an operational threat — warning that any tanker calling at a Saudi Red Sea port risks being attacked and threatening to close a second strait — and President Trump vowed to 'take care of' the group; the Washington Post reported a Red Sea blockade threatens a genuine new front alongside Iran's Hormuz closure. On a tenth consecutive night of US strikes, Iran's Revolutionary Guard said it hit two ships in the Strait of Hormuz plus targets in Bahrain and Jordan, and claimed a strike on Amazon infrastructure in Bahrain; Brent crude held above $90. At home, the Washington Post reported the Pentagon is 'quickly running short on cash' after sinking billions into the war, even as it admitted nearly 100 US service members wounded in two weeks (a day after the NYT reported dozens of injuries were withheld) atop 17 dead — the military named the Iraq fatality as Sgt Michael Swinton, and the WSJ reported the Jordan missile struck US troop housing. Mediators pushed a specific 10-day ceasefire as NBC warned of a 'quagmire'; Israel's Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich said Israel has 'no interest' in joining, though CNN reported it is readying contingency plans. A bipartisan disquiet grew in Congress, where Democrats hunted a mechanism to 'end it now' and some Republicans said the war should have been 'finished' already.

stats.label: Killed in Iran (Iranian figures)
stats.note: US/Israeli estimates put Iranian military deaths alone above 6,000

stats.label: US service members killed
stats.note: Per an ABC News/Time tally in a war fought largely in the air; includes those killed at Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase — where the WSJ reports the Iranian missile struck US personnel housing — and Sgt Michael Swinton of Fayetteville, NC, killed in Iraq during a 'controlled detonation' of a downed Iranian drone

stats.label: US service members wounded
stats.note: The Pentagon admitted nearly 100 US troops have been wounded over two weeks of escalating strikes, 21 July — a day after the New York Times reported dozens of injuries had been withheld

stats.label: Left without water in southern Iran
stats.note: Iranian figure after US strikes on water infrastructure, 18 July

stats.label: Iranian naval vessels destroyed or damaged
Global oil supply disruption
stats.note: Hormuz carries roughly a fifth of global oil flows

stats.label: Crude oil price
stats.note: Held above $90 a barrel — its highest of the war; Brent for September delivery rose ~2.7% in early Asian trade on 21 July after the tenth night of strikes and a fresh tanker fire in Hormuz

stats.label: US gasoline price
stats.note: US average gasoline topped $4 a gallon on 20 July (per ABC News); diesel has passed $5 — the war's cost reaching American drivers

stats.label: Strait of Hormuz status
stats.note: Declared closed by the IRGC on 12 July; now a live shooting zone — US striking to force it open, Iran attacking tankers to keep it shut. On 21 July the IRGC claimed attacks on a tanker and two other ships, one crew forced to abandon ship, two tankers ablaze

stats.label: Houthi blockade of Saudi Arabia
stats.note: Declared 20 July; on 21 July Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis warned tankers calling at Saudi Red Sea ports risk attack and threatened to close a second strait (per NPR and the Guardian). Trump vowed to 'take care of' the group — a second maritime front alongside Hormuz

stats.label: IAEA safeguards access
stats.note: Iran declared normal safeguards 'legally untenable' after strikes on Natanz; on 18 July it also suspended its commitments to the interim deal with the US

stats.label: Bushehr nuclear power plant
stats.note: Satellite images show fresh damage from strikes dated 7–12 July; Bushehr is Iran's largest nuclear plant

actors.members: United States, Israel; Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait and Bahrain after Iranian strikes; support from UK, Jordan, Qatar, Turkey and others
Iran (Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, President Pezeshkian), Hezbollah, Houthis, Popular Mobilization Forces, Hamas, Palestinian Islamic Jihad
